Notice Of Motion And Motion For A Stay Of Proceedings During Pendency Of Appeal
Off Calendar Jan-23-2025 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES: RE: DEFENDANT SHLOMO RECHNITZ, TOM WOOD, KRISTINE GUILLERMO, GEORGIA OTTERSON, ZORAIDA MARTINEZ, GINA FIZULIC, SAN MATEO HEALTHCARE & WELLNESS CENTRE, LP DBA, LOS ANGELES NURSING HOMES, LLC (FKA BRIUS, LLC), PACIFIC HEALTHCARE HOLDINGS, INC., ROCKPORT ADMINISTRATIVE SERVICES, LLC, ROCKPORT HEALTHCARE SUPPORT SERVICES, LLC, SAN MATEO WELLNESS GP, LLC Notice Of Motion And Motion For A Stay Of Proceedings During Pendency Of Appeal.

No appearance was made by any Party, Counsel, or Interested Person. Defendants San Mateo Healthcare & Wellness Centre, LP et al.'s motion to stay this action
pending appeal is granted and this case is stayed pending the conclusion of the appeal of the order denying the motion to compel arbitration. Plaintiffs Sean O'Connor and Michelle O'Connor allege that Defendants were neglectful and negligent in caring for Sean, eventually resulting in his death at a skilled nursing facility. (Complaint, 27-36.) Michelle, as Sean's successor-in-interest and in her individual capacity brought claims for elder abuse, negligence, constructive fraud, statutory violations, negligent infliction of emotional distress (NIED), wrongful death, and loss of consortium. This court denied Defendants' motion to compel arbitration, and Defendants have appealed that decision. Defendants now move for an order staying all trial court proceedings pending the appeal. Under the FAA, an interlocutory appeal denying a motion to compel arbitration automatically stays the proceedings. (Coinbase, Inc. v. Bielski (2023) 599 U.S. 736, 744; 9 USC 16(a).) In contrast, CCP 1294(a) provides that an appeal of "[a]n order dismissing or denying a petition to compel arbitration [does not] automatically any proceedings in the trial court during the pendency of the appeal." The parties disagree over whether federal or state law applies to Defendants' stay request. Defendants argue CCP 1294(a) is preempted by the FAA because it frustrates the purpose of the Act. Plaintiffs argue that CCP 1294(a) is not preempted because it is a procedural rule like CCP 1281.2. The court need not address the parties' disagreement because the parties' arbitration agreement expressly incorporates the FAA's procedural rules. Section 1.8 of the agreement provides: "This agreement shall be construed and enforced in accordance with and governed by the Federal Arbitration Act and the procedures set forth in the Federal Arbitration Act shall govern any petition to compel arbitration." (Decl. David Ries in support of Motion to Compel Arbitration, Ex. B, p.2.) Because the parties expressly incorporated the FAA's procedural rules, CCP 1294(a) does not apply even if it is procedural. Defendants' motion to stay the entire action pending resolution of the appeal is granted per 9 USC 16(a). (Coinbase, Inc. v. Bielski (2023)
599 U.S. 736, 744.) Moreover, even if the stay issue was governed by CCP 1294(a), the court would exercise its discretion to stay this case pending conclusion of the appeal of the order denying the motion to compel arbitration. Because of the uncertain and evolving state of arbitration law as it pertains to the issues on the motion to compel arbitration and the relative even balance of harms to the parties if a stay is or is not granted, the court believes that the better course, and the one consistent with the policies underlying the federal and California arbitration statutes, is for the Court of Appeal render a decision before the parties proceed further.

Judge: Harold Kahn. Clerk: Robert Woods. Not Reported. (301/HK)

MOTION TO DISMISS
Off Calendar Jul-30-2024 Denied
MINI MINUTES:

Hearing Re: DEFENDANT SHLOMO RECHNITZ, TOM WOOD, GEORGIA OTTERSON, ZORAIDA MARTINEZ, GINA FIZULIC, SAN MATEO HEALTHCARE & WELLNESS CENTRE, LP DBA, LOS ANGELES NURSING HOMES, LLC (FKA BRIUS, LLC), PACIFIC HEALTHCARE HOLDINGS, INC., ROCKPORT ADMINISTRATIVE SERVICES, LLC, ROCKPORT HEALTHCARE SUPPORT SERVICES, LLC AND SAN MATEO WELLNESS GP, LLC's MOTION TO DISMISS.

No appearances made in this matter. The Court adopts its tentative ruling.

Defendants San Mateo Healthcare & Wellness Centre, LP, et. al's motion to dismiss for forum non conveniens is denied.

Decedent Sean O'Conner resided in defendant's skilled nursing facility in Burlingame from April to September 2023. There, he allegedly developed bed sores and pressure injuries from defendants' lack of care. O'Conner's health declined and he passed away on September 25, 2023. His wife and son filed this elder abuse action in San Francisco County. Defendants move to dismiss under CCP 418.10(a)(2) for forum non conveniens.

Defendants argue that San Mateo County is a more convenient location than San Francisco County. But the forum non conveniens inquiry turns on whether a suitable, alternative forum outside of the state of California exists, and whether the litigation should proceed there. (See Cal-state Business Products & Services, Inc. v. Ricoh (1993) 12 Cal.App.4th 1666, 1670 [holding CCP 410.30 and 418.10 "are the means by which a defendant may challenge a plaintiff's selection of California as the forum for the litigation between them"].) The cases Defendants cite confirm this. (Stangvik v. Shiley Inc. (1991) 54 Cal.3d 744 [Sweden and Norway]; Century Indem. Co. v. Bank of America (1997) 58 Cal.App.4th 408, 411 [Hawaii]; Great Northern Ry. Co. v. Superior Court (1970) 12 Cal.App.3d 105 [Washington State].) Defendants cite no authority, and the court is aware of none, where CCP 418.10(a)(2) has been invoked on grounds that a change of venue from one California county to another would promote convenience. Instead, Defendants' authorities on intra-state venue changes rely on other statutes such as CCP 397. (See Mem. 7:3-4, citing J.C. Millett Co. v. Latchford-Marble Glass Co. (1959) 167 Cal.App.2d 218.)

In sum, because Defendants invoke an improper procedural tool, their motion to dismiss for forum non conveniens under CCP 418.10(a)(2) is denied.

Counsel for the prevailing party is to prepare and submit a proposed order which repeats verbatim the court's ruling and attaches a letter stating compliance with CRC 3.1312.
